title: Spinning rolags from hand cards
subtitle: Carding a washed fleece into rolags and spinning a lofty woollen-style yarn
excerpt: Hand cards produce a cylindrical rolag from washed fleece locks. Rolags are the traditional preparation for woollen-draw spinning: the fibres are arranged in all directions, producing a lofty, elastic yarn with air between the fibres. This entry covers loading the cards, forming a rolag, and spinning from it.
sourceNotes: Hand-carding technique synthesised from traditional British spinning practice. The rolag method is the standard beginner fibre preparation taught in UK spinning guilds.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft cylinder of fibre made by rolling carded wool off the hand card. The fibres inside a rolag lie in all directions, which produces a lofty, woollen-style yarn when spun from the end.

A spinning method in which the fibre is drafted from a rolag with the hand further from the orifice, allowing twist to enter the drafting zone. Produces an elastic, lofty yarn with more air between the fibres than worsted-draw.

The length of an individual fleece lock from cut end to tip. Hand cards work best with fibres between 4 and 10 cm staple; fibres shorter than 4 cm often card into neps, and fibres longer than 10 cm are better combed.

content.text: is the softest, most beginner-friendly fibre preparation. Every British spinning guild starts new spinners on rolags from hand cards because the preparation is straightforward, the carding process is meditative, and the resulting yarn has a warm, lofty character suited to cushions, outerwear, and blankets. You need washed fleece with a
between 4 and 10 cm. Corriedale, Romney, and BFL are the easiest starting breeds.

attrs.title: Wool dust
attrs.body: Carding throws fine wool dust. Work in a ventilated room and damp-mop the floor afterwards. Those with sensitive lungs should wear a dust mask.

content.text: Hold one card in the left hand, teeth facing up. Open a small lock of fleece and lay it across the teeth without pushing it down between them; it should rest lightly on the tooth tips. Load about a tablespoon-worth of fibre across the whole card surface. Do not overload; thin even loads produce better rolags than thick irregular ones.

Take the second card in the right hand, teeth facing down. Draw it lightly across the loaded card from the lower edge to the upper edge in a single pass, transferring some fibre to the right-hand card. Repeat until the fibre on both cards looks even and fluffy, with no visible clumps. Three to five passes is usually enough for pre-washed fleece. More passes break the fibre and produce short neps rather than long, even rolags.

Transfer the fibre onto one card. Place the second card face-up on a flat surface. Roll the fibre off the loaded card by pressing the backs of the two cards together and rolling them toward you; the fibre will form into a loose cylinder. Slide this cylinder off the card teeth. This is the
. It should be fluffy, even in diameter, and about 15 cm long.
Attach the rolag to your drop spindle leader. Use a

: hold the rolag loosely in the supply hand with about 10 cm between the supply hand and the spindle, and draw back with the front hand to draft the fibre while allowing twist to enter the zone. Spin from one end of the rolag through to the other, then join the next rolag with an overlap splice.

items.problem: The rolags contain lots of small hard neps (tangles)
items.cause: Over-carding, or using fibre with too short a staple length
items.fix: Reduce carding passes to three. If the fibre staple is under 4 cm, switch to a drum carder or supported spindle, as hand-carding very short fibre produces neps regardless of technique.

items.problem: The yarn breaks at every join between rolags
items.cause: The overlap splice is too thin or too short
items.fix: Keep a 5 cm tail at the end of each rolag and a 5 cm beginning at the start of the next. Overlap both tails, moisten slightly, and let the twist lock them before continuing to draft.
